data.title: Unity Bancorp: Quality At A Decent Price
data.description:
As somebody who is curious by nature, I am always looking for new and interesting opportunities to dig into. This ultimately led me to come across a bank by the name of Unity Bancorp (UNTY). With a market capitalization as of this writing of $401.7 million, the institution is certainly on the small side of the spectrum. However, I really prefer to analyze banks that are anywhere from $200 million to $5 billion, so this was a natural fit for me. Digging into the enterprise, I can see that shares are a bit more expensive than I would like them to be on a price-to-book and price-to-tangible book basis. However, the consistent growth the enterprise has achieved in recent years, combined with its high asset quality and low price-to-earnings multiple, makes it a compelling opportunity. At the end of the day, I do believe that this is a good ‘buy’ candidate for those that are interested in banking prospects.

A small bank to consider

If you're not familiar with Unity Bancorp, the company operates as a small bank holding firm incorporated out of New Jersey. From its headquarters, the business operates 21 bank branches that are mostly located along the Route 22/Route 78 corridors in parts of New Jersey and in Northampton County, Pennsylvania. The business also has access, including through its banking locations and its online platform, to clients in the New York City metropolitan area.

Over the years, the company has operated like many other small banks have. Most of its revenue currently comes from net interest income that it generates from the spread between the interest it gets on its loans and securities, and how much it has to pay out to depositors and lenders. Naturally, this means that the company takes on deposits. And using those funds, it originates commercial, SBA, consumer, mortgage, home equity, and personal loans. It also lends for the commercial and residential construction industries. On the non-interest side of things, the enterprise also generates revenue from stop-payment fees, wire transfer fees, debit card income, interchange fees, foreign ATM fees, and more.

Over the last few years, management has done a fine job of growing the firm from a balance sheet perspective. From 2022 to 2024, deposits expanded from $1.79 billion to $2.10 billion. Whenever I look at banks, especially small banks, I like to see how much of their deposit growth has come from brokered deposits. These are deposits that are lent to the company by other financial firms. There is no guarantee that they will be there for the business tomorrow and they carry higher interest rates than other forms of deposits. Small banks use them in order to grow, but they are a costly way to grow compared to organic deposits. Over the same three years that I looked at already, brokered deposits grew from $154.6 million to $217.9 million. If we strip this out from the equation, we would get total deposit growth from $1.63 billion to $1.88 billion. That is still an impressive showing as I see it.

This expansion on the deposit front has empowered the company to grow other parts of its balance sheet also. From 2022 through 2024, loans on the company's books expanded from $2.08 billion to $2.23 billion. The largest chunk of its loan portfolio includes the $1.09 billion dedicated to commercial real estate. This excludes $130.2 million of commercial real estate construction loans. The company also boasts $630.9 million in residential mortgage loans, as well as small amounts of SBA loans, consumer loans, other types of commercial loans, and residential construction loans.

Other aspects of the balance sheet also deserve attention. From 2022 through 2024, the company has seen the value of securities on its books remain in a fairly narrow range of between $135.7 million and $145 million. Cash and cash equivalents managed to rise from $114.8 million to $180.4 million. Over the same timeframe that we are talking about, it is noteworthy that debt on the company's books actually contracted from $393.3 million to $230.8 million. This is always nice to see and should prove accretive to the company's bottom line.

When it comes to the income statement, we have seen consistent growth in recent years. Over the last three years to be precise, net interest income expanded from $85.8 million to $94.7 million. This is in spite of the fact that the firm's net interest margin fell from 4.40% to 4.16%. This kind of contraction is not shocking to me. At a time when interest rates are elevated, banks have to compete with not only each other, but also alternative investment opportunities, for deposits. This usually results in a decline in net interest margin.

Historically, only a small portion of the company's revenue has come from non-interest activities. Examples here include interchange fees, debit card income, and more. During the window of time that is relevant to this analysis, Unity Bancorp saw its non-interest income expand slightly from $8 million to $8.5 million. This growth, combined with the rise in net interest income, allowed overall net profits to expand from $38.5 million to $41.5 million. This is not rapid growth by any means, and it can be attributed to higher operating expenses. But in this kind of environment that we have seen in recent years, I would take any growth over no growth.

When it comes to valuation, shares are on the cheaper end of the spectrum relative to earnings. In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced on a price-to-earnings basis. The 9.7 reading that the business boasts is near the high end of the 6 to 10 range that I like to see in this industry. However, as the chart also shows, only one of the five companies that I compared the business to is cheaper than it is on this basis. The opposite is true when we are talking about both the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. In the chart below, you can see how the enterprise stacks up against these similar firms. In fact, it ended up being the most expensive of the group.

Normally, this would be a turn-off as far as I am concerned. But I do make exceptions when asset quality is high. In the first chart below, you can see the company's return on assets. The reading of 1.68% that the business has is high on an absolute basis and is higher than any of these other firms that I compared it to. In the subsequent chart below that, you can see the same thing regarding return on equity. The 14.99% that the company has is comfortably higher than any of the other five comparable businesses. This demonstrates asset quality that is worthy of attention.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I must say that I am a fan of Unity Bancorp. Shares are attractively priced relative to earnings, though they are a bit pricey when it comes to book value and tangible book value. Asset quality is elevated, and the business has seen consistent growth from a revenue perspective, a profit perspective, and a balance sheet perspective. Due to these factors, I have no problem rating the company a ‘buy’ at this time.

data.title: SentinelOne: One Of The Better Growth Names To Buy Today
data.description:
SentinelOne (NYSE:S) has not been immune to the tariff-driven selloff. Wall Street may have been disappointed by management’s conservative guidance for this upcoming fiscal year. It appears that the low valuation has offered some downside support, with the net cash balance sheet also helping to sweeten the deal. SentinelOne has thus far not been able to capitalize on CrowdStrike’s (CRWD) outages but at these valuations, I see the stock performing quite well even so. I am upgrading the stock to a strong buy rating.

SentinelOne Stock Price

I last covered SentinelOne in September where I rated the stock a buy on account of the potential long-term benefits of the Lenovo deal. The stock has fallen around 33% since then.

The more attractive valuation has led to a ratings upgrade as growth investors can seek to take advantage of the ongoing market volatility.

SentinelOne Stock Key Metrics

SentinelOne is a cybersecurity stock focused on endpoint protection. CRWD is the giant in the space, though SentinelOne has tried to frame itself as being more AI-powered with more automated remediation.

The latest quarter saw the company deliver 29% YoY revenue growth to $226 million, exceeding guidance for $222 million.

The company saw its non-GAAP operating margin finally turn positive, albeit ever so slightly, at 1%.

I note that while the company has made meaningful progress on improving non-GAAP profitability, it has not made so much progress on a GAAP perspective. Even so, the progress on non-GAAP margins is still valuable as it means that the company is not burning cash.

SentinelOne ended the quarter with $1.1 billion of cash and investments versus no debt, representing a strong net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management has guided for the company to see 22% YoY revenue growth in the first quarter and 23% YoY growth for the full-year. I suspect that many investors were disappointed by this guide, as there may have been some hope for a more moderate deceleration in growth rates or even an acceleration given the CRWD incident.

On the conference call, management did note that their “competitive win rates were strong and improved compared to prior quarters,” but it is disappointing that this has not shown in the numbers. Management did note that their guidance was partly influenced by their decision to retire their legacy deception solution with about “$10 million of expected churn.” Excluding this impact, the full-year growth outlook might improve to around 24%. Management also appeared to repeatedly emphasize the conservatism of the guidance, calling it a “good achievable starting point.”

The stock had previously rallied on news of a partnership with Lenovo. Management noted that this partnership is still in the works, with the projected impact expected to be “more back end loaded” and likely “in the next fiscal year.” Investors often like to have something to look forward to in the future, and it is nice to have this Lenovo catalyst on hand for the medium term.

Is SentinelOne Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, SentinelOne traded at just around 5.6x sales.

That multiple is a steep discount to its closest competitor in CRWD, which trades at around 20x sales and other cybersecurity names like Palo Alto Networks (PANW) and Zscaler (ZS), which trade at around 12x sales. In my view, the main difference appears to be both lower profit margins as well as the more narrow product offerings. The company has already achieved positive cash flow generation and I expect an inflection in GAAP profitability over the next 3 years. Executing against margin expansion looks like a clear catalyst for a material re-rating. My valuation target is 9x sales, based on 30% long term net margins and a 30x earnings multiple. From current levels, the stock looks poised to deliver considerable upside in a bullish outcome, with strong returns coming from both ongoing top-line growth (around 20% annually) and multiple expansion (around 50% upside to 9x sales). Sure, GAAP operating losses may offset this, but I estimate the impact as being just around 4% to 5% annually.

SentinelOne Stock Risks

It is possible that SentinelOne is unable to effectively compete against CRWD and others. If this were to occur, not only would the growth story fall apart, but the company may also fail to achieve satisfactory profit margins. SentinelOne might need to widen its product portfolio simply to stay relevant - this process might involve diluting shareholders through expensive M&A. There may be some political risk as the company was recently targeted by the Trump administration, eventually leading to the resignation of senior officer Chuck Krebs. The stock is not “dirt cheap” here and may experience considerable volatility in a market downturn.

SentinelOne Stock Conclusion

The recent market volatility has created attractive buying opportunities for brave investors. SentinelOne looks like a solid pick, as the valuation allows for strong returns as the company executes against boosting profit margins. SentinelOne embodies what I look for among high-quality growth names for the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio, and that explains why it is a large core holding. I am upgrading the stock to a strong buy rating.

data.title: Big Mac Vs. CPI: An Alternative View Of U.S. Inflation
data.description:
Inflation and the Price of a Big Mac

As you probably know, the Big Mac index is usually used to compare purchasing power in different countries. However, I think the Big Mac index can also be applied to compare the purchasing power of Americans in different years and use it to estimate official inflation data. So, in this article, I decided to compare Big Mac prices in the US and compare them to the US inflation rate. What I am using is not really a Big Mac index in the usual sense, but will be focused exclusively on the US.

Below you can see a table where I calculated the increase in the price of a Big Mac since 2000 and compared it to the U.S. Consumer Price Index. And you know, the Big Mac has been increasing in price almost all the time at twice the rate of inflation. From 2000 to 2025, the Big Mac has increased in price by 163%, while inflation has cumulatively increased by only 85%.

Such a difference rightly raises the question of how accurate the inflation data are. After all, it is no secret that many people, despite the impressive growth of the economy over 25 years, do not feel richer. And in this contest, I recalculated US GDP, both nominal and per capita, in Big Macs. And what you can see in the table below.

If we count GDP in Big Macs instead of the U.S. dollar, the average American is poorer in 2024 than in 2000. And America's total GDP has grown only slightly over the years, from 4.58 trillion Big Macs in 2000 to 4.88 trillion Big Macs in 2024. Moreover, from 2000 to the pandemic, the economy calculated in Big Macs declined.

Technological Deflation

And here I will try to explain what happens to inflation as I understand it.

Imagine an iPhone 12 in 2020, its base model cost $799. A year later, a new iPhone 13 came out, its price was the same, $799. That said, the iPhone 12, as an older model, likely dropped in price.

In this case, I think inflation calculated that iPhone prices fell because the iPhone 12 got cheaper. The bottom line is that this approach puts downward pressure on inflation.

This effect is called hedonic adjustment.

"Hedonics is the science of trying to work out how much product quality has changed and adjusting inflation to take account of the fact that more expensive products are not just inflation, but also improved quality. It is used in the US and UK calculation of inflation."

And it turns out that at the expense of hedonic adjustment, almost all technological development turns against inflation by underestimating its indicators. And in this connection, we can refer to such an indicator as CPI for Computers, Peripherals, and Smart Home Assistants. This indicator is almost always declining!!!

In the table below, you can see that this index has declined since 2007 from a level of 100 points to 34 points in 2024. And I think this effect is just directly related to the example I described with the iPhone.

This effect, I believe, doesn't just apply to smartphones, but to many other products, all the way down to cars. New products get better over time, but are often sold at the same price as past models. And statistics turn this technological development against inflation in favor of deflation.

So Big Mac, as odd as it may seem, is largely right. And the feelings of people who don't think they've gotten richer over the last 25 years are largely correct.

The Evolution of Inflation Calculation Methodologies and Their Implications

I'm not the only person who has noticed this. There are many who think the same way, and even consider alternative inflation, which is counted using old methodologies when hedonic adjustments were not yet included in the inflation calculation.

Before the 1990s and early 2000s, the methodology for calculating inflation was different. It puts more emphasis on calculating the value of a fixed consumer basket of goods. But modern methodologies have changed.

Inflation now takes into account many additional factors.

Substitution Bias: accounts for consumers switching to cheaper counterparts when prices rise.

Hedonic Adjustment: adjusts prices for improvements in the quality of goods.

Changing Basket of Goods and Services: regularly updates the basket of goods to reflect changing consumer habits.

Owners' Equivalent Rent - OER: uses estimated rents instead of asset prices when measuring housing costs.

And the Big Mac index, which is rising in value at twice the rate of official inflation, clearly shows that current methodologies are flawed, and their imperfections lead to systematic underestimation of inflation.

Economic Growth and Welfare

Against this background, if one considers what I have written on inflation to be fair, one can change one's view on the economy and one's expectations a great deal.

I think that GDP growth at the level of 2% in real terms, taking into account the official inflation rate and GDP deflator, from this point of view, is not growth at all, but stagnation. Growth by a smaller amount means recession. And only when the economy grows by 3% or more does it truly grow.

The entire period from the 2008 crisis to the pandemic can be called a stagnation and crisis. But that period was mitigated by the Fed's loose monetary policy. And with this assessment, those investors and economists who like to count economic cycles can make adjustments. Because the picture of economic cycles will be completely different.

In one of my articles, I wrote that credit in the U.S. is now at a low base, which, if the Fed cuts the Fed Funds rate, could cause a strong credit growth and lead to a new credit growth cycle. And from this angle, if you exclude the trade war that Trump started, the US economy has a huge potential for growth, because if the growth of the economy since 2008 has been less than is believed, then the economy is on a different economic cycle, not at its peak!

In the same way, one can feel differently about the Fed's policy. The stated inflation target of 2% is not a low level at all; in real terms, it could be 4%. And if so, that level is much easier to achieve than it may seem. And part of the reason for this may be that the average P/E in the US has risen since the 90s. After all, under current conditions, it turns out that already at 4% real inflation, the Fed's policy will be very soft. The distortion of inflation makes the adjustment in Fed policy towards easing, because with higher actual inflation, for example, at 4%, the Fed will act as it would with inflation equal to 2%, which would be according to official data.

Yields on bonds and on deposits from this perspective will look completely different. After all, if you discount the rate of inflation by, say, 2%. A level that, I assume, is underestimated in official inflation every year. Even now, 10-year U.S. Treasury bonds have a negative real yield adjusted for inflation. Their yield is now 4.3%, while official inflation was 2.4% according to the latest data. And if we add another 2% to that, real inflation could be as high as 4.4% per annum, which is higher than the yield on 10-year Treasuries. And such a statement of the question completely changes the position of the U.S. national debt. After all, if Treasury bonds have negative yields in real terms, then the problem of the U.S. national debt simply does not exist.

From this position, ordinary people's attitudes toward deposits and other fixed-income instruments may be reconsidered. Because often the interest will not cover the real loss of purchasing power.

And even the value of a company's shares can be looked at differently. Below I have compiled a table where I have recalculated the values of the S&P500 index in value to Big Mac.

And in this picture, the dizzying market upswing of the last 25 years is no longer there.

Summary

Of course, my estimate of a 2% annual inflation distortion is hardly correct either. But the point is that methodologies for calculating inflation are imperfect. And because of this, you have to realize that official inflation does not accurately reflect its real level, which can make many issues look different from what they really are. And I think that this factor should always be kept in mind. It may help you to look at all economic issues from a different angle, and help you to find answers to many questions.

Let me say right away that I do not think that this is done on purpose. After all, our life is multifaceted; there are always many processes in the economy, and it is very difficult to take everything into account. Therefore, in the issue of inflation accounting, it is most likely about the imperfection of inflation calculation methods.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Vroom: Resulting Subprime Auto Lender Is Still Losing Money Hand Over Fist
data.description:
From day one, I had been bearish on Vroom, Inc.'s (OTC:VRMMQ) business model, as the idea of selling used cars 'door-to-door' was fundamentally flawed, since it locks Vroom into lower gross margins than its peers. Furthermore, the company was spending money like a technology company, but its business model is more akin to CarMax (KMX). In my most recent note from September 2023, I predicted that the company would eventually reach its "intrinsic value of $0 in the next few quarters".

Unfortunately for shareholders, I was proven correct, as Vroom filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in November 2024. However, interestingly, Vroom's stock appears to be making a comeback after its bankruptcy declaration, with Vroom shares quintupling to ~$33 / share in recent days (Figure 1).

Are there any positive developments at the company worth highlighting, and is it a turnaround candidate?

Upon closer inspection, the new Vroom appears to be a deep subprime auto lender with average yields north of 20%. However, with elevated defaults and realized losses, Vroom's auto lending business is still deeply unprofitable.

Without more details on how the company plans to become profitable, I continue to recommend investors stay away from Vroom shares.

Brief Company Overview

Vroom, Inc. was a 'disruptive' e-commerce platform for selling used vehicles. Vroom pioneered the 'door-to-door' transaction model with "no-haggle pricing and contact-free at-home pick-ups and deliveries".

However, after its recent bankruptcy filing, Vroom discontinued its e-commerce operations and now markets itself as a "leading indirect automotive lender serving the independent and franchise dealer market nationwide" through its United Auto Credit Corporation (UACC) subsidiary. Vroom also provides AI-powered analytics and digital services for automotive retail via CarStory.

End Of The Road For Flawed Business Model

In the end, it was not hard to see why Vroom's e-commerce business model was destined to fail. As I have explained in the past, Vroom operated at a fundamentally lower gross profit per unit because it had to incur 'inbound transportation costs' and 'outbound transportation costs' to ship vehicles to and from its regional hubs compared to traditional used car dealers where customers bring their vehicles to brick-and-mortar stores.

Unless Vroom can convince consumers to sell their cars to Vroom at significantly less than intrinsic values (which is unlikely since consumers can easily get a competitive quote from a local used car dealership), Vroom must 'eat' the transportation costs and report lower gross profit per vehicle.

That is why Vroom only generated Vehicle Gross Profits of $594 / vehicle in 2023 (Figure 2), compared to typical used car retailers like CarMax with ~$2,000 gross profits per vehicle.

Furthermore, Vroom was not built like a used-car dealership with a focus on efficiency and productivity. In 2023, Vroom incurred $166 million in compensation and benefits to sell 17,401 vehicles, or $9,500 in compensation and benefits to sell each vehicle (Figure 3). In contrast, CarMax paid $1.2 billion in compensation and benefits to sell 766k vehicles in fiscal 2024 (February year-end, so roughly equivalent timeframe to Vroom), or $1,600 per vehicle.

Bankruptcy Was The Right Call

In my opinion, Vroom's decision to discontinue its e-commerce operations was the right call. Paying nearly $10,000 in compensation to sell a vehicle worth $30,000 (Vroom's average vehicle revenue) was clearly an unsustainable proposition.

However, with the discontinuation of the e-commerce platform, is the remaining business still viable? On the surface, Vroom's remaining businesses, UACC and CarStory, appear to be very high gross margin businesses. Looking at Vroom's 2023 annual report, Product and Finance revenues totaled $209 million in 2023 versus just $35 million in COGS (Figure 4).

If SG&A can be shrunk to match the leasing and digital operations, the resulting business could be highly lucrative. The potential of this leasing business is probably why Vroom shares have leapt in the days post the bankruptcy filing.

Under Vroom's bankruptcy filing, the company entered into a Restructuring Support Agreement ("RSA") with lenders where $290 million of unsecured convertible senior notes were converted into equity. Each note holder received new common stock equal to 75% of the face value of notes, assuming a per-share value of $9.14.

Vroom's restructuring agreement also threw old shareholders a bone, as the RSA will allow existing holders of Vroom common stock to exchange each share for one share of new common stock and warrant to purchase a share of new common stock with a strike price of $12.19 and exerciseable for 5 years.

However, to be clear, prior shareholders were effectively wiped out by the Chapter 11 bankruptcy. Adjusted for reverse stock splits, Vroom's latest share price of ~$34 / share is less than 1% of the company's IPO price of $4,000 back in 2020 (Figure 5).

What Does New Vroom Look Like?

For speculators interested in the company and investors holding Vroom shares, what does the new Vroom look like? We got a taste recently when the company reported Q4 and 2024 full year results.

Looking at the company's income statement, 3 things stood out to me (Figure 6).

First, compensation, professional fees, and IT expenses were a combined $124.4 million for fiscal 2024 (+2.9% YoY) and $29.3 million for Q4/24 (-4.6% YoY). Recall from Figure 3 above, total expenses for these 3 line items were $222.6 million in 2023, so we did see a dramatic and significant reduction in expenses. This is encouraging.

The second interesting point is Vroom's interest income of $48.7 million for Q4/2024 and $201.8 million in 2024. From the company's balance sheet, we note that total finance receivables were $822.1 million in 2024 compared to $852.2 million in 2023 (Figure 7).

This implies Vroom earned $202 million in interest on average receivables of $837 million, or a 24% average yield! I normally don't keep tabs on auto loan interest rates, but the 24% yield is quite shocking and suggest Vroom's loans are made to the riskiest of borrowers. For example, a recent Nerdwallet article suggests only deep subprime auto loans have interest rates approaching what Vroom charges (Figure 8).

In recent years, as low-income consumers struggled with the post-COVID inflation crisis, subprime auto borrowers have been increasingly defaulting on their loans, with the 60+ day delinquency rate reaching a record 6.6% in recent months (Figure 9).

Subprime auto lending can be a very lucrative business if the risk of default is priced properly. However, they can also be a money sink if lenders do not have a firm grasp of their customer's creditworthiness.

Unfortunately, Vroom does not appear to be a good lender. From Figure 6, we note that Vroom experienced $32 million in realized losses in Q4/24 and $120 million in the full year 2024. Based on the company's average loan balance of $837 million in 2024, this implies a default rate of ~14.3%, which is far in excess of the subprime delinquency rates mentioned above.

So after accounting for interest expenses, loan losses, and SG&A, Vroom's 'continuing' operations lost $30.7 million in Q4/24 and $131.8 million in 2024!

CarStory Is A No Story

The other part of the Vroom business, CarStory, appears to be a rounding error upon closer inspection. While Vroom touts CarStory as "a leader in AI-powered analytics and digital services for automotive retail", the business only generated $12.3 million in revenues in 2024, and actually shrank YoY (Figure 10).

After accounting for expenses, CarStory barely generated $0.9 million in 'adjusted EBITDA' for the full year 2024. For most other listed company, a business of CarStory's size would not be significant enough to warrant its own business segment reporting.

Conclusion

Although Vroom's Chapter 11 bankruptcy has cleaned up its balance sheet, I do not believe the 'new Vroom' is a viable turnaround candidate. Vroom's third-party lending business, UACC, appear to be a deep subprime auto lender, charging average interest rates far north of 20%. However, due to elevated realized losses, Vroom continues to operate at steep operating losses.

While the company has $57.5 million in cash and excess liquidity as of December 31, 2024, at the rate it is losing money ($30.7 million loss from continuing operations in Q4/24), I do not know how long the 'new Vroom' can survive. I continue to recommend investors avoid Vroom.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: My Highest Conviction Buy Right Now That I Am Buying Hand-Over-Fist
data.description:
Over the past decade, the S&P 500 (SPY) and gold (GLD)(IAU) have both delivered strong total returns.

Of course, the reasons for this have been different. In the case of the S&P 500, we have seen explosive growth from mega cap technology stocks as the likes of Apple (AAPL), Meta (META), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG), and Microsoft (MSFT) have emerged as money printing machines that are global forces on the technological stage.

Moreover, in recent years, the likes of NVIDIA (NVDA) and Broadcom (AVGO) have taken off as the AI boom has given tech stocks another major jolt higher with major anticipated future growth and profits for those companies that are most able to capitalize on this new disruptive technology.

Meanwhile, gold - which has been my highest conviction buy over the past year - has roared higher due to yawning budget deficits for leading economies of the world, as well as the collapse of the so-called peace dividend with the growing power of China and the willingness of countries like Russia, Iran, and China to challenge the existing status quo of a U.S.-led rules-based order. On top of that, recent changes even to U.S. foreign and economic policy have added further challenges to the existing global status quo. As a result, individual and institutional investors are now joining central banks around the world in stockpiling the yellow metal as a safe haven asset.

However, another major store of value, namely silver (SLV)(SIVR), has not participated in gains nearly to the same extent that stocks and gold have over the past decade. However, moving forward, I think that there will be a major shift in the current trend, and silver will be a massive outperformer in the coming years. As a result, it has now displaced gold as my highest conviction investment.

The Bull Case For My Highest Conviction Investment

The biggest reason why silver is such a high conviction investment for me right now is that it has historically been highly correlated with gold and in fact has often been much more volatile than gold and thereby generally rises more than gold does during precious metals bull markets. This was last seen during the big gold and silver bull market, a little over a decade ago.

However, in the current gold bull market, silver has lagged behind gold's performance:

Therefore, based on historic norms, I expect silver to play catch up moving forward and to slingshot higher in the near future.

Another reason I am so bullish on silver is that major new technologies use it a lot, including solar panels, conductors and connectors, batteries, chemical processes, medical devices, computers, and semiconductors. This provides a natural industrial demand base for the precious metal.

Additionally, while gold is generally preferred as a store of value to silver by central banks, and it is much easier and cheaper to store due to its higher value and smaller size per ounce, silver is still a historic store of value and medium of exchange that has been around since the earliest years of recorded history (as evidenced by repeated references to silver as a popular form of money in the ancient book of Genesis among other Ancient Near Eastern references). Given that gold has shot up so much recently, silver likely will grow in popularity as an alternative.

This also extends to the jewelry industry where gold jewelry has become priced so expensively that silver likely will enjoy greater demand. Already there are accounts of Indians (a major consumer of gold jewelry) shifting away from higher karat gold jewelry in favor of lower karat pieces that are mixed with other metals (such as silver) due to the surge in gold prices.

Another reason to really like silver is that there is a large shortage of physical silver, as there are reportedly 5.5 paper claims for every physical ounce of silver available for delivery today. This means that the price of silver is being artificially suppressed by a huge amount of "paper silver" that is inflating the supply of silver in order to meet market demand, and this dynamic should eventually unwind itself, which would be an enormous tailwind for silver. This is a big reason why leading silver investors like Sprott are so bullish on it right now and expect it to soar higher.

You can also see silver’s status as a coiled spring ready to soar higher in its price ratio to gold, which indicates that silver is at one of its cheapest points in history, as the chart below illustrates. This is particularly unusual, because the ratio tends to go lower during bull markets for gold and silver, yet today the exact opposite is happening:

Last but not least, I think silver will serve as a powerful alternative safe haven in an environment where government deficits remain high and appear likely to remain so for the foreseeable future. There is growing risk of explosive geopolitical conflict involving either China and Taiwan or the Philippines, North Korea versus South Korea, and/or Iran and Israel. Therefore, I think that silver is an attractive risk-adjusted option because it should thrive during a boom scenario due to its industrial base demand or in a negative case scenario for inflation and/or geopolitical conflicts due to its status as an undervalued safe haven relative to gold.

Investor Takeaway

When you combine all these factors together - its relative recent underperformance to gold, its rapidly growing industrial demand, its illustrious history as a store of value, its increasing attractiveness as a jewelry alternative to gold, the large shortage it currently faces in the market, its low historical valuation, and its potential to serve as a highly demanded safe haven should a major war break out, I am very bullish on silver right now. As a result, I am loading up on it via ETFs, miners, and physical bullion, including through an opportunity to generate substantial yield on silver bullion in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Oddity: Rare Beauty Gem
data.description:
Oddity (ODD) is not your average beauty retailer. It is a digital native beauty player that bypasses classical retailers and sells directly to consumers with outstanding profitability and efficiency. The key to the business model is the use of artificial intelligence that’s used to match users with products and tests products through live consumer trials before new lines are launched. This unique go-to-market model is an underappreciated growth driver that will continue driving healthy returns and superb unit economics. My analysis points to 62% upside by 2028.

Overview

Oddity’s modern, internet-native approach to beauty retailing and wellness dates back to its launch in 2018 even though the company was founded by two siblings in 2013. The idea was radically different than anything else in the market back then. They intended to sell beauty products online while using technology to do what store assistants usually do. From day 1, they intensely focused on data gathering with every customer completing a quiz, which is often the case with modern and disruptive direct to consumer models like Hims & Hers (HIMS) and many others. Equipped with data-rich insights from the quiz, the system is continuously getting better at predicting what each person needs.

The first product launch, Il Makiage, grew very fast as customer satisfaction was very high, and the business model was pioneering. Soon after, Oddity launched SpoiledChild, a second brand focused on haircare sing the same strategy. To my surprise, all product development happens in-house with new formulas tested in blind trials and then launched if the results of the trials are superior to market leaders. The internal expertise took a leap in 2021 when Oddity acquired a biotech lab in Boston for $76 million. The lab specialized in the development of new molecules that fuel innovation for the next generation of products for the brand. For a company that was founded in 2013 and launched D2C operations in 2018, the speed of reaching vertical scale and control is quite rare. This business has above average retail margins, high recurring revenues, and control over its supply and innovation. It is also built for the online world and not the legacy, inventory boom and bust prone, shelf retailing.

The edge

To evaluate the potential and opportunity that lies within Oddity, it is important to understand its competitive edge. The foundation of its differentiation is the nature of the model and its control over the customer journey alongside their data. Unlike the classic retail beauty players that rely heavily on wholesale distribution, this company keeps the relationship direct and intimate. The 2+ billion of data points collected from over 60 million users, are feeding the recommendation engine and personalizing the experience in a way that no other beauty retailer can. The model is designed to drive high conversion, above average recurring revenues, brand loyalty, fast innovation and low product-return rates. In other words it’s structurally superior to its competition in many ways.

Although the company does a decent job marketing itself, it’s not all fluff. At a time when the cosmetics market is growing roughly 6% CAGR, Oddity has grown roughly 40% CAGR over the past 36 months. The model scaled from basically zero in 2018 to over $647 million in 2024. Importantly, this exponential growth did not come at the expense of profitability. The model is very profitable with gross margins in the 70s and operating margins in the high teens and growing. It’s a rare combination of an increasingly vertically integrated model, with high volumes and great margins.

Looking back in the history books of the beauty and retail business, a big curse tends to be inventory boom and bust cycles. They are a classic example of stock underperformance and a case in point is Estee Lauder (EL) that fell victim to this spell, and it wiped out 86% of its value since its peak in 2022. The reason behind this is the lack of control over the distribution channel and the hunger of misaligned middle-management to sell volumes at all costs. The result is inventory oversupply that leads to inventory flushes, price discounting, brand image impairment and long-term value destruction. In the case of Oddity, there is good reason to believe that the model structurally insulates them to a sufficient extent. There will inevitably be cyclicality in the model and that’s the nature of retail overall. However, beauty tends to be quite resilient and the intimacy of the customer relationship that Oddity owns wholly, will likely further insulate their revenue-base.

Another factor I like about this business is its positioning. They smartly identify themselves as a “beauty and wellness” brand. This opens doors for TAM expansion in the future. The company claims that the total addressable market of the overall beauty and wellness market is over $600 billion. The strength and intimacy of the customer relationship is evidenced by the cohort net retention rates that paint a strong picture of loyalty and mind share.

Growth and profitability assumptions

I forecast revenue to grow to $1.3 billion by 2028, implying a compound annual growth rate of roughly 19%. Execution on existing product lines thus far and net customer retention trends lead me to think that this is a realistic and highly plausible assumption. If anything, this assumption is probably conservative, especially taking into account that two new brands will be launching late this year or early next year. With even more experience in product launches, the company is well-equipped with insights to efficiently develop and roll out its products through blind testing and data-driven personalization. My assumptions imply a continuation of the current model at scale - 100% direct to consumer sales and zero channel diversification.

In terms of profitability, I made the conservative assumption of stable margins due to product development, marketing and scaling efforts to support new product launches. There’s good reason to believe that margins are likely to improve due to operational leverage, especially on the marketing front as data collection is a key strength of the model, but I keep it simple and model flat gross and operating margins.

Both, revenue and profitability assumptions are grounded in reality and the execution track record thus far. Even though this company is still in its infancy compared to the opportunity it is chasing, there’s good reason to believe that they are taking a measured approach to slowly expanding their product range to entice adjacent users and not alienate the existing customer base.

Valuation and risks:

In general, there’s healthy skepticism in the market about direct to consumer models after the COVID boom, but Oddity is proving to have a resilient model that resonates with consumer needs and preferences. The management team is executing on consistent growth, margin expansion, recurring sales and strong cash generation. In this context and the rationale exemplified in the above sections, my assumptions are rather conservative. The exit multiple is also close to the average of the range of 13-17x FWD EV/EBIT that the company has traded in the past, particularly when factoring in the superiority of growth that this business offers compared to the broader retail sector shown below.

In terms of what can go wrong, Oddity’s internet-native business model depends heavily on ad performance, particularly on platforms like TikTok and Meta. Although ad effectiveness is generally rising on these platforms, this cannot be taken for granted. Performance marketing is algorithm-dependent and execution prone. If performance turns poor, the unit economics of the business may deteriorate. It is encouraging to know that cohorts are sticky and spend healthily on the platform, but this is an area to continue monitoring.

Another risk to keep in mind is brand concentration and product fatigue, two common issues in beauty. Right now, two products are driving the entire business and as long as that is true, there’s concentration risk to take into account. The management team is preparing two new launches, and they have the track record to show that they are capable of successfully launching, but this effort requires a high-level execution. Consumer preferences shift fast and although this company is well-equipped with data to protect itself from ephemeral trends, there is always a risk.

Finally, the company has invested in Oddity Labs, a biotech initiative with the goal to innovate on the ingredient level. Even though this can offer further defensibility in the business model, the experiments are still in early stages and commercialization is yet to be proven. I will continue to monitor R&D spend and commentary around that part of the business.

data.title: 2 Absurdly Cheap REITs With An Average 6.3% Yield To Grow Your Retirement Income
data.description:
Introduction

The on and off again tariffs have wreaked havoc on the overall market the past few weeks. And the trade war between the U.S. and China is heating up with China now imposing 125% tariffs on U.S. goods, up from 84% previously.

As a result, the back and forth between the two countries will likely result in increased market volatility in the coming weeks. However, long-term investors should ignore the noise and continue to invest wisely.

For retirees, now is a great time to position your portfolio to take advantage of the dip in equities and grow your income stream in the process. Most retirees rely more on income and care less about growth as a result of age.

In this article, I list two insanely cheap REITs with sound fundamentals that investors, particularly retirees, should consider for their portfolios.

Why REITs Make Great Income Vehicles

Before getting into the two companies in this article, I wanted to talk about why REITs make good investments for retirees, particularly now with increased volatility.

As someone who considers themselves great at seeing and finding opportunities, now is an opportune time to build your income stream as a retiree. And REITs are where you should look.

For starters, if you're a retiree, you most likely are in search of higher yielding investments instead of growth because of your age. When you become older, life priorities shift, likely the same for your investments.

In the chart below, you can see REITs currently have much higher yields than stocks & bonds in the U.S., and globally. At this moment bonds are also attractive in the U.S. at 2.3%. But buying quality REITs that own iconic assets could be considered more attractive.

With President Trump pausing tariffs for 90 days with the exception of China, many are likely wondering what may happen next. Until then, I expect the market to remain volatile.

Tariffs are also expected to be highly inflationary, which may prompt the FED to keep interest rates elevated for much longer.

I do suspect we will see at least one cut this year, but this remains to be seen, especially if tariffs are reinstated in the coming months.

The good thing is real estate has been known to outperform during periods of high inflation as well.

And lastly, REITs have delivered strong returns and beat the overall stock & bond markets since 1991. As most know, REITs do have periods of underperformance, specifically more recently in 2022 when interest rates were hiked the fastest in history.

But other sectors can have periods of underperformance as well, depending on what's going on in the overall market or economy.

But the key is to find quality REITs with strong fundamentals that will likely continue to reward you with income.

This may slow during times of uncertainty, but these two REITs I expect will continue to cover their dividend, at least for the near to medium term.

Without further ado, let's get into the two REITs retirees should consider.

#1 American Assets Trust (AAT) - 7.35% Yield

The first REIT is one I think doesn't get enough love or coverage, American Assets Trust. AAT is based in San Diego, California and owns office and mixed-use assets.

Since the pandemic, the office sector hasn't been seen as very attractive, many still recovering from the unexpected crisis.

I last covered AAT back in February. And since then, the REIT has sold off, down over 20%. This is in comparison to the S&P (SP500), who's down roughly 15% over the same period.

With a yield well over 7% backed by a solid payout ratio of roughly 69%, now may be a great time to pick up some shares.

They were also able to grow their funds available for distribution from $133.4 million to $140.4 million year-over-year, solid considering the challenging economic backdrop.

Although office REITs are considered riskier with companies still allowing employees to work from home, impacting their occupancies, AAT's property ownership in affluent markets like San Diego & Hawaii is what makes them particularly attractive.

During 2024, AAT delivered a solid performance despite economic headwinds for REITs with higher for longer interest rates. In Q4, they beat on both top & bottom lines. For the year, the REIT brought in a record $2.58 in FFO, their third consecutive year.

And despite 2025 expected to be a challenging year with an expected decline in FFO to $1.87 - $2.01, this is still more than enough to cover their annualized dividend of $1.36, which they raised 1.5% in early February.

2026 is expected to be a better year with the stabilization of one of their San Diego properties and future developments projected to provide $0.30 bottom-line growth once 93% occupancy is reached.

American Assets Trust is also targeting growth in their multifamily portfolio, providing diversification away from the office sector.

In late February, AAT acquired a 192-unit apartment community in San Diego for $67.9 million that was 93% leased using available cash on hand. Additionally, their balance sheet was solid, with no remaining debt this year and none maturing until 2027.

This allows AAT to continue making investments and gives them some flexibility should interest rates remain higher for longer.

Additionally, their forward P/FFO ratio of just 9.70x using their 2025 midpoint of $1.94, this also makes them attractive.

Despite 2025 likely continuing to be a volatile year, limiting their upside, 2026 is likely to be much better, with management expecting $0.30 accretion to their bottom line.

Until then, investors get paid a 7.35% yield well-covered by cash flows while they wait for potential upside.

#2 Rexford Industrial (REXR) - 5.18% Yield

Keeping it in Southern California is Rexford Industrial Realty. As a result of tariffs, REXR and peers have sold off drastically since my last thesis, tripling the S&P's drop of 10% at the time of writing.

This is a result of them owning and developing warehouses, which store and ship goods and are usually sourced from countries outside the U.S.

But their sell-off should be viewed as an opportunity. To be clear, I think office and industrial REITs are riskier than say retail REITs like Agree Realty (ADC) and Realty Income (O).

But their fundamentals remain strong and provide significant upside over the long term. And REXR is no exception. Despite headwinds, the REIT was able to grow rental revenue from $207.9 million to $239.7 million year-over-year.

What also was impressive is they managed to grow both core FFO & AFFO despite a sizable decline in same-store portfolio occupancy. This declined from 97.1% to 94.1% over the same period.

So, if investors are worried about the REIT's dividend safety, this was well-covered with a 73% payout ratio.

The company also raised their dividend by a solid 3% in February to $0.43 a share, remaining committed to dividend growth. And although dividend growth may slow until headwinds abate, their yield and price drop make them particularly attractive.

For 2024, acquisitions totaled $1.5 billion. And despite no pipeline, Rexford does expect to see 1% NOI growth from redevelopment and repositioning projects in 2025.

Guidance has been established at $2.37 - $2.41, a growth rate of 2.1% from 2024's $2.34. Moreover, management established a $300 million share buyback program, which I expect them to take advantage of REXR's price drop.

At the current price, the REIT has a forward P/FFO multiple of just 13.73x. This is well-below the industrial REIT average of 20x FFO. Currently, their upside over 40% and well-covered dividend yield makes them worth considering at current levels.

Their balance sheet also supports their dividend with a leverage ratio of 4.6x. Their debt was also well-staggered and robust liquidity available.

This included nearly $1 billion available on their credit facility and $400 million in cash and unsettled forward equity expecting to be settled by the upcoming quarter.

Risks

As a result of tariffs, recession fears have picked up in recent weeks. If one does come to a fruition, both REITs could see further downside as this would likely cause occupancies to drop and a decline in M&A activity.

Moreover, both portfolios are concentrated in Southern California, making them riskier investments as SOCAL is known for natural disasters like earthquakes and wildfires.

This also subjects them to higher costs and regulatory challenges, which may be viewed as risky for potential investors. This is something investors should keep an eye on going forward.

Conclusion

Despite both AAT & REXR being in riskier sectors that expose them to further downside from future tariffs, their strong fundamentals, yields above 5% and well-covered dividends make them attractive for retirees looking to grow their income.

Moreover, you get paid well while you wait for headwinds to abate and could potentially see significant upside, likely over the medium to long term.

While 2025 will likely provide further volatility, investors should consider taking advantage of the dips in both American Assets Trust and Rexford Industrial Realty's share prices.

data.title: Bicara: Innovative Precision Tumor Targeting
data.description:
Many therapies in oncology show initial promise, but fail to meet the twin hurdles of tumor resistance and toxicity. It remains the central challenge to find approaches that thread this needle - with potent efficacy and a manageable safety profile. Against this demanding backdrop, Bicara Therapeutics (BCAX) presents a compelling early-stage investment consideration. I believe that Bicara's focused strategy around its lead bifunctional antibody candidate ficerafusp alfa is a scientifically differentiated approach with significant potential for use in hard-to-treat cancers such as Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma.

Bicara isn't boiling the ocean. It's focused on one rational biological mechanism: Targeting the epidermal growth factor receptor and neutralizing transforming growth factor-beta directly within the tumor microenvironment. In this dual-action design, embodied in ficerafusp alfa, EGFR inhibition targets the tumor cell directly while dismantling the immunosuppressive shield of TGF-beta. This localized two-pronged assault may overcome resistance mechanisms to single-target therapies and provide an environment favorable to immune-mediated tumor destruction.

Financial Update

Bicara's financial position provides an overview of its operational status and runway following its transition to a public company. The company had $489.7 million in cash and cash equivalents as at December 31, 2024. This is a big jump from $230.4 million at the end of 2023, helped by its IPO proceeds. Importantly, on the basis of its current operational and development plans, Bicara projects this cash reserve will cover its activities through the first half of 2029 and give it a large runway to advance its clinical programs.

Reflecting the ramp-up of clinical activities, including the pivotal FORTIFI-HN01 trial initiation, research and development expenses increased. For the fourth quarter in 2024, R&D expenses were $19.9 million, compared to $10.6 million in 2023. For the full year, R&D spending was $63.6 million in 2024 versus $30.6 million in 2023. The increase was largely driven by costs associated with initiating the large Phase 2 / 3 trial and advancing work on the ongoing Phase 1 / 1b study of ficerafusp alfa.

For the fourth quarter and the full year ended on December 31, 2024, G&A expenses were $6.8 million and $18.8 million respectively. This is compared to $3.1 million versus $9.3 million for the same periods in 2023. It grew mainly because of increased personnel costs and professional fees related to public company readiness and operations. Thus, the company's net loss widened to $21 million for the fourth quarter and $68 million for the full year in 2024, from $12.4 million and $52.0 million for the same periods in 2023 (noting the 2023 full-year figure included a non-cash expense related to preferred stock valuation).

Dual-Threat Strategy

Ficerafusp alfa is the core asset and the first-in-class bifunctional antibody. Such a molecule combines an anti-EGFR antibody component with a TGF-B trap (extracellular domain of the human TGF-B receptor II). A well-validated target in many cancers including HNSCC, EGFR promotes tumor cell proliferation and survival. Therapies focusing exclusively on EGFR, for example, cetuximab, are often resistant and lack durability. TGF-b plays an important immunosuppressive role in the tumor microenvironment and inhibits the attack of cancer by T-cells and natural killer cells.

By physically linking the EGFR blockade and TGF-B neutralization, ficerafusp alfa targets its dual activity at the EGFR-expressed tumor site. In this targeted delivery, efficacy is maximized while systemic toxicities of less targeted TGF-B inhibition may be minimized. Preclinical work showed that this strategy could outperform anti-EGFR antibodies alone in preventing tumor recurrence and restoring immune activation. The human clinical studies test this hypothesis. Mainly focused on an ongoing Phase 2/3 trial of ficerafusp alfa in combination with pembrolizumab as the first-line therapy in recurrent/metastatic HNSCC in the HPV-negative population. The dose expansion cohorts in the Phase 1/1b study continue to explore its utility in other settings, including squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal and possibly other squamous cell tumors.

Series C Upswing

The $165 million Series C financing announced in December 2023 was a significant milestone. Beyond the large capital infusion, timing was critical. That follows the presentation of positive interim data from the Phase 1/1b study of ficerafusp alfa at major medical conferences such as the American Society of Clinical Oncology and the European Society for Medical Oncology earlier in the year. These presentations demonstrated clinically meaningful anti-tumor activity and a tolerable safety profile in the challenging first-line, HPV-negative R/M HNSCC population combined with pembrolizumab. All this new capital from top-tier investors was earmarked to accelerate the development of ficerafusp alfa and initiate pivotal trials.

Key Conference Data

Bicara has presented encouraging, early clinical data at major oncology meetings. In interim data from the dose expansion cohort of ficerafusp alfa plus pembrolizumab in first-line R/M HNSCC, the company presented interim data at ASCO 2023. The results revealed promising anti-tumor activity mainly in HPV-negative patients with poor prognosis. A June 2024 update with longer follow-up maintained this trend with a 64% overall and 18% complete response rate in patients with HPV-negative tumors compared to historical pembrolizumab monotherapy rates. Median progression-free survival was 9.8 months, and the median duration of response and overall survival were not reached, suggesting durable responses. Similarly, data presented at ESMO 2022 showed the combination was well tolerated and active. Data presented at the ASCO GI Symposium in January 2025 showed a 25% confirmed overall response rate in heavily pre-treated advanced squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal patients with pembrolizumab. These consistent updates with encouraging efficacy and manageable safety instill confidence in the therapeutic concept.

Pivotal Trial Launch

A major step forward was recently announced: the first patients enrolled in FORTIFI-HN01. It is the pivotal Phase 2/3 trial of ficerafusp alfa plus pembrolizumab versus pembrolizumab plus placebo in first-line R/M HNSCC patients with HPV-negative tumors expressing PD-L1 (CPS ≥1). The initiation of this registration-enabling trial marks a turning point in the development of ficerafusp alfa from exploratory to potential market approval. Success in this trial may establish a new chemotherapy-free standard of care for this high unmet need population.

Upcoming Risks and Competitors

Investment in clinical-stage biotechnology involves inherent risks. The primary risk for Bicara is clinical trial failure. Promising Phase 1 / 1b data aside, larger, randomized Phase 2 / 3 trials are needed to establish efficacy and safety, and many drug candidates fail here. It also involves regulatory risk. Approval by bodies like the FDA requires robust data indicating a good benefit-risk profile. Biologic manufacturing scale-up can be challenging.

Interestingly, although ficerafusp alfa has a relatively unique dual-targeting mechanism, other companies are exploring EGFR inhibitors, TGF-b inhibitors, or different combination strategies in HNSCC and other solid tumors. Those existing treatments - chemotherapy and checkpoint inhibitors such as pembrolizumab and nivolumab - constitute the current standard of care against which Bicara must demonstrate superiority. Other bispecific antibodies to different combinations are also under development by competitors, although Bicara's EGFR/TGF-b approach seems to be unique. Its success is dependent on ficerafusp alfa demonstrating clear advantages over these alternatives in efficacy, durability, or safety.

Gauging Market Potential

Market research reports estimate that the overall HNSCC market size was between $1.0 and $1.8 billion in 2023/2024 and could grow to $2.4 to $2.8 billion by 2029-2034. Specifically, the recurrent/metastatic market was worth around $1.6 billion in 2024 and was expected to reach $3.8 billion by 2035. HPV-negative HNSCC constitutes a significant proportion of these cases, especially in those with poorer prognoses that are routinely treated in the first-line setting for recurrent or metastatic diseases. The incidence rates suggest tens of thousands of new addressable patients per year in both the US and Europe.

Treatment costs for advanced HNSCC are high. Immunotherapies like pembrolizumab and targeted therapies like cetuximab may cost upwards of six figures per patient annually. Studies have quoted annual costs broadly, with some analyses citing costs exceeding $70,000-$100,000 a decade ago and modern combination therapies often much higher. The market for the ficerafusp alfa combination is potentially sizable if a future price point is competitive with existing branded oncology biologics (conservatively, $180,000 per patient per year).

This translates to 7,500 patients if we assume an average of 30,000 addressable first-line R/M HPV-negative HNSCC patients annually in the US and major EU markets, and Bicara achieves a plausible peak market share of 25% in this segment. That equates to a potential peak annual revenue of $1.3 billion at this first indication of $180,000/year. A fraction of the larger HNSCC market or expansion into other EGFR/TGF-b-driven tumors (such as certain lung or colorectal cancers studied preclinically) would significantly increase this potential. It is an extremely speculative calculation and dependent on clinical success, regulatory approval, pricing, and market adoption, but it illustrates the enormous commercial potential of ficerafusp alfa if and when it succeeds.

Concluding Thoughts

Bicara Therapeutics is a targeted, scientifically rational investment case within the high-risk/high-reward biotech sector. As its lead asset, ficerafusp alfa uses a differentiated dual-targeting mechanism to overcome key resistance pathways in solid tumors. With substantial venture rounds and a successful IPO, the company is well-funded to pursue its pivotal trial in first-line R/M HPV-negative HNSCC. Good early clinical data implies meaningful activity with manageable safety in combination with pembrolizumab. Clinical and regulatory hurdles remain significant, but the market opportunity for HNSCC alone is huge. Investors who want exposure to newer oncology approaches with high upside potential should watch Bicara Therapeutics closely as its pivotal data develops.

data.title: Oil Bulls Should Pay Close Attention To Forum Energy Technologies
data.description:
Forum Energy Technologies, Inc. (FET) is a global manufacturer of products for the oil and gas, industrial and renewable energy industries. It operates two segments - Drilling and Completions and Artificial Lift and Downhole. The former segment sells capital equipment and consumable products used in the drilling process, subsea remotely operated vehicles, hydraulic fracturing pumps, cooling systems, high-pressure flexible hoses and flow iron, wireline cable and coded tubing. The latter segment manufactures and supplies products and solutions for the artificial lift, well construction, production and infrastructure markets. Basically anything to do with the operation of oil wells that the general public has no clue about, FET is involved in it. This is not a business that would be considered a hot and growing market by most investors. But what this company lacks in an engaging story it has in spades with its ability to generate cash flow. This makes it a good value play candidate for investors who are bullish on oil and like the speculative upside potential and volatility of a microcap stock. As one can see, FET has historically been quite volatile, to put it nicely:

Reviewing the company's full-year results, 2024 revenue was $816.4 million, a 10.5% increase over 2023's revenue of $738.9 million. The gross margin grew 24.8% from $204.2 million to $255 million year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA grew 49% from $67.1 million to $100 million. FET booked a net loss of $135.3 million for 2024 compared to a net loss of $18.9 million in 2023. But that variance is explained by a $119.1 million impairment in the coiled tubing product line. While FET doesn't look that great from an EPS perspective, its true value comes from its ability to generate cash flow. Operating cash flow grew from $8.2 million in 2023 to $92.2 million in 2024. The company generated free cash flow of $105.1 million in 2024 compared to $1.6 million in 2023. So FET is trading at less than 2x its trailing FCF.

This outstanding ability to generate cash flow has enabled FET's Board of Directors to approve an aggressive $75 million share repurchase program. $2 million worth of shares were repurchased in January 2025. Couple that with net insider buying and FET has a tight float with the company and insiders having the ability and incentive to make that float even tighter.

If there is a black cloud hanging over FET, it's the stagnant growth that was masked by the acquisition of Variperm, a specialist in thermally compliant downhole completions systems & tools, in January 2024. This stagnation becomes more apparent when looking at results by segment:

Drilling and Completions revenue was down 6.5% from 2023 to 2024 while Artificial Lift and Downhole revenue increased 46% on the strength of the acquisition. The Artificial Lift and Downhole segment has far superior margins, generating $48.9 million in operating income in 2024 compared to $17.8 million for the Drilling and Completions segment despite having less revenue. If the company can continue this trend towards generating revenue in the higher margin Artificial Lift and Downhole segment, gross margins will continue to improve, which will have a positive impact on the bottom line.

However, this lack of growth becomes more apparent when reviewing 2025 guidance as the year-over-year comparison is apples to apples. Full year 2025 adjusted EBITDA is expected to be in the $85 to $105 million range while free cash flow is expected to be in the $40 to $60 million range. The midpoint of guidance results in a 5% decline in EBITDA from 2024 while free cash flow is expected to drop by more than half. Order backlog dropped from $241.6 million at the end of 2023 to $213.5 million at the end of 2024. However, bookings, which consist of written orders or commitments for FET's products or related services increased to $780.3 million at the end of 2024 versus $724.3 million at the end of 2023. So there is mixed data related to how FET will fair in 2025, but don't expect a spectacular year relative to 2024.

Conclusion: Value investors who are bullish on oil should consider an investment in FET

FET is a cheap stock with a lot of potential to do well, but is far from perfect. Its suite of products makes it imperative to the energy industry. While the company remains optimistic about the industry in the long term, it's clear from its guidance that it believes 2025 will be a mediocre year. This is why I say FET is an appropriate investment mainly for oil bulls. If an investor is going to invest in energy companies anyways, they may consider allocating some of that cash to FET as opposed to a producer. If the oil business is robust in 2026 and beyond, FET will see strong growth. I believe that FET is well positioned to outperform producers in an energy bull market. However, for someone who is not bullish on oil, they can see FET's strong cash generating ability but will also see its flat organic growth and be less impressed.

While the Board may have approved a $75 million stock repurchase plan, in reality FET won't buy anywhere near that amount of shares in 2025. FCF is expected to be only around $50 million and the CEO stated that half of the FCF generated in 2025 will be used to repay debt. As interest expense of $31.5 million in 2024 was a big driver of the variance between EBITDA and net income, this is a wise move. Investors shouldn't expect such aggressive buying of stock in 2025, but should expect the company to be in a better position to report positive EPS in 2026 upon a reduction in interest. A combination of a low FCF along with positive net income should finally get the stock moving higher. Assuming the oil business is in a neutral to bullish environment. I am placing a speculative buy on FET but will remain on the sidelines for now until I have a more bullish outlook on the energy sector.

data.title: Accenture: Buy The Trump Slump (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

Accenture plc (ACN) has been a target of the DOGE and as a result, the stock has plunged recently. I believe this is a massive overreaction that creates a buying opportunity. As I will discuss below, key technical bottoming signs have appeared and so a bullish reversal is likely moving forward. As for the fundamentals, Q2 earnings were respectable. Guidance for their Q3 and full year 2025 showed off the company's confidence in their outlook as strong resilience is expected despite Trump headwinds. While revenue growth is expected to remain relatively stable in USD terms for their Q3 and the company has raised their guidance on select metrics for the full year, the P/S ratio has plummeted since its high earlier this year. This suggests undervaluation of the stock. As a result, I initiate Accenture at a buy rating on the basis of technical bottoming and fundamental resilience.

Technical Analysis

The Basics

Trends, Support & Resistance

Accenture currently remains in a downtrend that began back in late February. This trend line seems to have pressured the stock significantly this month and may continue to do so moving forward. The sole support level in this timeframe is at around 280. This was the level of a bullish engulfing pattern that occurred lately. As you can see, there have been numerous candle wicks in this area. Three resistance levels are currently above the stock, of which the nearest is at around 300. This was a downside gap earlier this month and seems to have already acted as resistance once. The next source of resistance is in the mid-310s. This level was supported in early to mid-March while contributing to resistance very early this month. Lastly, the mid-320s is likely to be significant resistance with that level being the major late March gap down that occurred on overwhelming volume.

Moving Averages & Indicators

The 50-day SMA had a death cross with the 200-day SMA just very recently, showing long-term pessimism in the stock. The deviation between the two lines continues to grow and so downward momentum is still building. The stock also broke beneath the 200-day SMA's support in early March, which should have been a signal of things to come. The latest crossover for the EMAs was back in late February, and it was a bearish one. The 9 day EMA has been consistently below the 21 day EMA ever since, demonstrating sustained bearishness. The gap between the EMAs has at least stayed relatively steady and so near term downward momentum hasn't been accelerating.

Accenture stock broke beneath the Bollinger Band's midline in late February as a signal of weakness. Recall, that the downtrend started back then as well. The midline seems to have contributed as resistance very early this month as a confirmation signal of the downtrend. For the MACD, there have been numerous non-meaningful crossovers lately showing indecision in the stock. The histogram has also been very flat. The major signal for the MACD is that the indicator has been recovering since its late March low while the stock has continued to drop. This is a sign that strength is building underneath the surface and so there is positive divergence. For the RSI, the indicator now has a reading of 35.05 after being below 30 earlier this month. The bears have therefore lost some ground to the bulls. The RSI also now seems to be in a bit of an uptrend that suggests further strength. Therefore, there is positive divergence here as well with two consecutive higher lows in the indicator. Lastly, the stochastics just had a bearish crossover as the %K's trajectory has turned negative again. While this shows a stalling recovery in the stock, both lines still remain above 20 as a sign of weakened bears. The stochastics also shows bullish divergence with its low this month holding significantly above its March low.

Takeaway

The chart shows Accenture being in a strong downtrend and the SMAs just flashed a death cross. These are worrying signs, but I believe there are reasons to be hopeful if you're a bull on the stock. The bullish engulfing pattern identified above and the positive divergence in the MACD, RSI, and stochastics suggest that a bullish reversal is likely to occur. The bottom may be in or near as the indicators have seen broad strengthening for some time. While it may be more risky to buy in before the downtrend is broken, I believe brave investors may be rewarded for jumping in with only these bottoming signals to work with.

Fibonacci

Fan Lines

Using the early February peak and the early April low as the anchor, resistance fan lines are given in the chart above. As you can see, during the recent bounce in the stock, the 23.6% fan line may have contributed significantly to resistance. Multiple candlesticks were rejected by this fan line in a short period of time. If a bullish reversal is to continue, the stock must break this fan line quite quickly as the stock may be pressured below the bullish engulfing pattern in the near future. The 38.2% fan line is also now moving near 300 and so there are still obstacles for Accenture even if a bullish reversal does occur.

Projection

Using the early February high again, the late March low, and the retracement to around 320, the Fibonacci Projection has provided potential support zones as shown above. The nearest level at 61.8% is very near the 250 price level in the stock. Therefore, if the bullish reversal fails and the declines continue, this area may represent a strong support level or floor for the stock moving forward. The next projection level at 100% is quite far below and so a break below 250 would be a concerning sign for the bulls if it does, in fact, happen.

Ichimoku Cloud (Advanced)

Accenture stock is far below the cloud currently, and so general bearishness is quite strong at this point. The Leading Span A also crossed below the Leading Span B in early April and so the cloud itself is also bearish now. Furthermore, as the Leading Span A continues to fall into late May, resistance may be worrying about the stock as the cloud is also thickening substantially. Therefore, it could get increasingly difficult for Accenture to break above the cloud moving forward. The Conversion Line crossed below the baseline in late February to show that near term momentum had shifted bearish. The deviation between these two lines has decreased a bit since late March, however, and so downward momentum seems to have slowed modestly. Lastly, the Lagging Span is currently significantly below the stock price of early March, but its gap with the historic stock price has decreased since mid-February. Therefore, the Lagging Span confirms the downtrend, but it is increasingly evident that bearish momentum is on the decline.

Fundamental Analysis

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Accenture reported their FY2025 Q2 earnings back on March 20th and showed, in my view, respectable results. The company posted revenues of $16.7 billion, up 5% YoY and 8.5% in local currency terms. This figure exceeded consensus expectations by $49.70 million, showing strength in the business. As you can see in the chart above, growth did slow QoQ as the growth recovery shows some signs of stalling. Another key figure is their Generative AI bookings. For their Q2, they recorded $1.4 billion in new bookings, up significantly from the prior year period's $600 million figure. This shows AI is driving substantial growth for the company. For EPS, they reported an adjusted figure of $2.82, up 2% YoY. This figure beat estimates slightly by $0.01. Operating cash flow saw healthy increases as they generated $2.85 billion versus the year ago quarter's $2.10 billion. As shown above, the 35.81% growth rate is still a deceleration from the previous quarter, however. Nonetheless, I believe the business remains overall healthy with both revenue and operating cash flow growth remaining resilient.

Guidance

For their FY2025 Q3, they are expecting to see revenues ranging from $16.9 billion to $17.5 billion. In Accenture's local currency, revenue growth is expected to be between 3% and 7%. The midpoint at 5% represents a slowdown from Q2's 8.5% growth rate. In USD terms, the midpoint of $17.2 billion is an 4.2% increase over the prior year period's $16.5 billion in revenue. This is a minor deceleration versus Q2's 5% growth rate in USD terms. Operational growth is therefore expected to moderate slightly for the company. While I won't go into depth for their full-year guidance, there are some notable developments. In their Q2 report, they raised their local currency revenue growth guidance to between 5% and 7% from 4% to 7% and also raised their EPS guidance to between $12.55 and $12.79 from $12.43 to $12.79. This indicates strengthened confidence over the longer-term outlook for the business. Note that this guidance comes as the U.S. government is threatening spending cuts on consulting. Therefore, Accenture is demonstrating high resilience as these guidance figures indicate that they are in a strong position to weather these headwinds.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

The P/S ratio has collapsed since its 2025 high earlier in the year. The multiple was at levels of over 3.6 and now only sits at 2.67. I believe this is a massive overreaction that makes the stock undervalued at current levels. As discussed above, revenue growth in their Q2 has dropped QoQ and the growth recovery seems to be stalling. However, the figure remains near average levels relative to the past few years. Furthermore, Q3 guidance only shows a very minor deceleration for revenue growth in USD terms. In addition, Accenture actually raised their full-year local currency revenue growth guidance despite the U.S. Government's threats of spending cuts. Therefore, the outlook shows strong resilience in their business moving forward. Despite this, the P/S ratio has plummeted to its lowest level since early calendar 2023, showing that the current pessimism in the stock is overdone. Currently, the Information Technology sector has a median P/S of 2.52 and so Accenture is at a mild premium to peers. Given their strong position in the consulting industry, I believe the company can easily justify an even greater premium.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, key technical bottoming signals were highlighted. The bullish engulfing pattern along with the positive divergence signals in the MACD, RSI, and stochastics show that the bottom is likely in for the stock. As for the fundamentals, most recent earnings were respectable. Furthermore, guidance showed resilience as revenue growth is expected to remain relatively stable for Q3 in USD terms. Guidance for local currency revenue growth and EPS was also raised for the full year, demonstrating a confident management team despite Trump headwinds. While this is the case, the P/S ratio has shown largely irrational contraction as investors seem to have overreacted to the news. With the selloff coming to an end according to the technicals and the fundamental setup now being attractive, I initiate Accenture at a buy rating.
